texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6.1 Observing Objects Around Us
texts.text: 6.1 Observing Objects Around Us

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ghulan and Sheeta are thrilled to go to their new class after the summer break. They enter their classroom and start talking. "What have you brought to school today?" asks Sheeta.
texts.text: Ghulan and Sheeta are thrilled to go to their new class after the summer break. They enter their classroom and start talking. "What have you brought to school today?" asks Sheeta.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: After some time, Madam Vidya, their science teacher, enters the class and starts interacting with the students to draw their attention to many things they use in their daily life. She asks, "How are they similar to or different from each other? What are their shapes and colours? How does it feel when you touch them? Are some of them heavier than
texts.text: After some time, Madam Vidya, their science teacher, enters the class and starts interacting with the students to draw their attention to many things they use in their daily life. She asks, "How are they similar to or different from each other? What are their shapes and colours? How does it feel when you touch them? Are some of them heavier than

texts.label: text
texts.orig: others?" All things are made up of some materials like paper, wood, cloth, glass, metal, plastic, clay, and so on.
texts.text: others?" All things are made up of some materials like paper, wood, cloth, glass, metal, plastic, clay, and so on.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Any substance that is used to create an object is referred to as material.
texts.text: Any substance that is used to create an object is referred to as material.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Based on everyday observations, one can conclude that objects are made up of various materials.
texts.text: Based on everyday observations, one can conclude that objects are made up of various materials.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The earliest pottery found in the Indian subcontinent dates back to 7,000 to 8,000 years in the Ganga plains (Lahuradewa) and in Baluchistan (Mehrgarh). About 4000 BCE onwards,
texts.text: The earliest pottery found in the Indian subcontinent dates back to 7,000 to 8,000 years in the Ganga plains (Lahuradewa) and in Baluchistan (Mehrgarh). About 4000 BCE onwards,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Sindhu-Sarasvati developed techniques of wheelturned pottery production, pigmentation, application of protective or decorative coats (called 'slips') of multiple colours, decorative painting, etc. These techniques became further sophisticated during the Sindhu-Sarasvati (also known as 'Harappan') Civilisation (2600-1900 BCE), with a bright red surface painted
texts.text: Sindhu-Sarasvati developed techniques of wheelturned pottery production, pigmentation, application of protective or decorative coats (called 'slips') of multiple colours, decorative painting, etc. These techniques became further sophisticated during the Sindhu-Sarasvati (also known as 'Harappan') Civilisation (2600-1900 BCE), with a bright red surface painted

texts.label: text
texts.orig: designs geometric patterns, and aquatic and terrestrial animals. The clay used for making pots, dishes, bowls and other items was carefully selected __ ) wheel and finally baked in kilns (baked clay is called 'terracotta'). Pots were used for various purposes, from cooking to storage of food grains, oil, ghee, and so on. Some very large storage jars and other pottery items are exhibited at the National Museum, New Delhi.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You must have noticed that an object can be made from different materials and some materials can be used to make more than one object.
texts.text: You must have noticed that an object can be made from different materials and some materials can be used to make more than one object.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The method of arranging the objects into groups is called classification. Objects can be classified on the basis of a common property that they have.
texts.text: The method of arranging the objects into groups is called classification. Objects can be classified on the basis of a common property that they have.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Similarly, we can classify materials based on certain properties.
texts.text: Similarly, we can classify materials based on certain properties.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The materials that are required to make a tumbler should be capable of holding water.
texts.text: The materials that are required to make a tumbler should be capable of holding water.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: What decides which material should be used for making an object? We choose a material to make an object depending on its properties and the purpose for which the object is to be used.
texts.text: What decides which material should be used for making an object? We choose a material to make an object depending on its properties and the purpose for which the object is to be used.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We may use different materials for making different parts of an object. For example, a pen may be made up of different materials like plastic, metal and ink.
texts.text: We may use different materials for making different parts of an object. For example, a pen may be made up of different materials like plastic, metal and ink.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6.3 What are the Different Properties of Materials?
texts.text: 6.3 What are the Different Properties of Materials?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let us learn some more properties of materials.
texts.text: Let us learn some more properties of materials.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6.3.1 Observe and identify appearance of materials
texts.text: 6.3.1 Observe and identify appearance of materials

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Materials often look different from each other. Freshly cut wood, which is unpolished, has a distinct appearance, quite different from that of iron. Similarly, iron looks different from copper or aluminium. However, there might be some similarities among iron, copper and aluminium that make them different from wood.
texts.text: Materials often look different from each other. Freshly cut wood, which is unpolished, has a distinct appearance, quite different from that of iron. Similarly, iron looks different from copper or aluminium. However, there might be some similarities among iron, copper and aluminium that make them different from wood.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let us doa sorting challenge! Collect small pieces of paper, cardboard, wood, chalk, copper wire, aluminium foil and any article made up of brass, bronze, steel, etc. Take a look at the pieces you have collected. Do any of these materials shine when light falls on them? Observe their texture (whether
texts.text: Let us doa sorting challenge! Collect small pieces of paper, cardboard, wood, chalk, copper wire, aluminium foil and any article made up of brass, bronze, steel, etc. Take a look at the pieces you have collected. Do any of these materials shine when light falls on them? Observe their texture (whether

texts.label: text
texts.orig: rough or smooth), colour and other noticeable features, and record your observations in the notebook. Group the collected pieces based on their appearance.
texts.text: rough or smooth), colour and other noticeable features, and record your observations in the notebook. Group the collected pieces based on their appearance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Materials that typically have shiny surfaces are said to have a lustrous appearance. Such materials with lustre are usually metals. Examples of metals include iron, copper, zinc, aluminium, gold, etc. However, some metals may lose their lustre and start to look dull or non-lustrous due to the effect of air and moisture on them. As a result, we often notice the lustre only on their freshly cut surfaces. Non-lustrous materials are those that do not have a shiny surface. Some examples of non-lustrous materials are paper, wood, rubber, jute, etc.
texts.text: Materials that typically have shiny surfaces are said to have a lustrous appearance. Such materials with lustre are usually metals. Examples of metals include iron, copper, zinc, aluminium, gold, etc. However, some metals may lose their lustre and start to look dull or non-lustrous due to the effect of air and moisture on them. As a result, we often notice the lustre only on their freshly cut surfaces. Non-lustrous materials are those that do not have a shiny surface. Some examples of non-lustrous materials are paper, wood, rubber, jute, etc.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Are all lustrous materials metals?
texts.text: Are all lustrous materials metals?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: "All that glitters is not gold" goes an old saying! Not all the materials that shine are metals. Surfaces of some materials are made shiny by polishing or coating them with thin layers of plastic, wax or any other material which makes them look shiny. These materials may not be metals.
texts.text: "All that glitters is not gold" goes an old saying! Not all the materials that shine are metals. Surfaces of some materials are made shiny by polishing or coating them with thin layers of plastic, wax or any other material which makes them look shiny. These materials may not be metals.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6.3.2 Which materials are hard?
texts.text: 6.3.2 Which materials are hard?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When you press different objects or materials with your hands, some of them like stones, may be hard to compress, while others, like an eraser, can be easily compressed. Take a metal key and use it to scratch the surface of a piece of wood, aluminium, stone, iron, candle, chalk and any other material or object. Can some materials be scratched more easily than others? Materials which can be compressed or scratched easily are soft, while other materials which are difficult to compress or scratch are hard. However, these properties are relative in nature. For example, rubber is harder than sponge but softer than iron.
texts.text: When you press different objects or materials with your hands, some of them like stones, may be hard to compress, while others, like an eraser, can be easily compressed. Take a metal key and use it to scratch the surface of a piece of wood, aluminium, stone, iron, candle, chalk and any other material or object. Can some materials be scratched more easily than others? Materials which can be compressed or scratched easily are soft, while other materials which are difficult to compress or scratch are hard. However, these properties are relative in nature. For example, rubber is harder than sponge but softer than iron.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You have learnt that materials can have different properties, like lustre, hardness, softness and colour. Can you think of any other properties that are shown by materials? Let us explore it further.
texts.text: You have learnt that materials can have different properties, like lustre, hardness, softness and colour. Can you think of any other properties that are shown by materials? Let us explore it further.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6.3.3 Explore materials through which one can see or cannot see
texts.text: 6.3.3 Explore materials through which one can see or cannot see

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ghulan, Sheeta and Sara are playing hide and seek with their friends. Ghulan hides behind a wall, Sheeta hides behind a big tree in the garden while Sara hides behind the frosted glass door (which has a hazy surface). Sheeta's younger brother can see all of this happening through a glass window of his house.
texts.text: Ghulan, Sheeta and Sara are playing hide and seek with their friends. Ghulan hides behind a wall, Sheeta hides behind a big tree in the garden while Sara hides behind the frosted glass door (which has a hazy surface). Sheeta's younger brother can see all of this happening through a glass window of his house.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The materials, through which things can be seen clearly, are called transparent. Glass, water, air, cellophane paper, etc., are some examples of transparent materials.
texts.text: The materials, through which things can be seen clearly, are called transparent. Glass, water, air, cellophane paper, etc., are some examples of transparent materials.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Why did Ghulan, Sheeta and Sara choose these places to hide?
texts.text: Why did Ghulan, Sheeta and Sara choose these places to hide?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Do you think it would be possible for Sheeta's brother to see her and her friends through a closed wooden window of the house?
texts.text: Do you think it would be possible for Sheeta's brother to see her and her friends through a closed wooden window of the house?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are many materials through which you are not able to see at all. These materials are called opaque. Wood, cardboard and metals are examples of opaque materials.
texts.text: There are many materials through which you are not able to see at all. These materials are called opaque. Wood, cardboard and metals are examples of opaque materials.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The materials through which objects can be seen, but not clearly, are known as translucent. Butter paper and frosted glass are examples of translucent materials.
texts.text: The materials through which objects can be seen, but not clearly, are known as translucent. Butter paper and frosted glass are examples of translucent materials.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lookat Fig. 6.5. Identify and label the nature of materials used by Ghulan (A), Sheeta (B), Sara (C) and Sheeta's brother (D).
texts.text: Lookat Fig. 6.5. Identify and label the nature of materials used by Ghulan (A), Sheeta (B), Sara (C) and Sheeta's brother (D).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6.3.4 What is soluble in water; what is not?
texts.text: 6.3.4 What is soluble in water; what is not?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ghulan was sweating when he came home after playing in the evening. He was feeling tired and thirsty. Ghulan's mother mixed a spoonful of sugar, a pinch of salt and some lemon juice in a glass tumbler of water and offered him this shikanji (lemonade) to drink.
texts.text: Ghulan was sweating when he came home after playing in the evening. He was feeling tired and thirsty. Ghulan's mother mixed a spoonful of sugar, a pinch of salt and some lemon juice in a glass tumbler of water and offered him this shikanji (lemonade) to drink.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ghulan noticed that while his mother was mixing sugar and salt in water, the salt and the sugar disappeared after a while.
texts.text: Ghulan noticed that while his mother was mixing sugar and salt in water, the salt and the sugar disappeared after a while.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let us try a simple activity to explore how different materials behave when we mix them in water!
texts.text: Let us try a simple activity to explore how different materials behave when we mix them in water!

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 6.7: Let us explore
texts.text: Activity 6.7: Let us explore

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Collect small amounts of sugar, salt, chalk powder, sand and sawdust.
texts.text: ¢ Collect small amounts of sugar, salt, chalk powder, sand and sawdust.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Take five glass tumblers and fill them about two-third with water.
texts.text: ¢ Take five glass tumblers and fill them about two-third with water.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Puta teaspoonful of sugar in the first glass tumbler, salt in the second one, chalk powder in the third, sand in the fourth and sawdust in the fifth glass tumbler.
texts.text: ¢ Puta teaspoonful of sugar in the first glass tumbler, salt in the second one, chalk powder in the third, sand in the fourth and sawdust in the fifth glass tumbler.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Write down your observations in Table 6.5.
texts.text: Write down your observations in Table 6.5.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You might have noticed that some materials completely disappear when mixed in water. We say that these materials dissolve in water or, in other words, they are soluble in water (Fig. 6.6a). Some materials do not mix with water and do not disappear even after we stir them for a long time. These materials are insoluble in water (Fig. 6.6b).
texts.text: You might have noticed that some materials completely disappear when mixed in water. We say that these materials dissolve in water or, in other words, they are soluble in water (Fig. 6.6a). Some materials do not mix with water and do not disappear even after we stir them for a long time. These materials are insoluble in water (Fig. 6.6b).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Water plays an important role in the functioning of our body because it can dissolve a large number of materials.
texts.text: Water plays an important role in the functioning of our body because it can dissolve a large number of materials.

texts.text: (a) Soluble material in water

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Make your own ORS!
texts.text: Make your own ORS!

texts.label: text
texts.orig: ORS—Oral Rehydration Solution— is used to treat dehydration due to diarrhoea or other illnesses. These ready-made ORS packets are available in primary health centres and also in the market. Each packet is dissolved in a litre of water before use. If these are not available, ORS can be prepared at home by mixing six teaspoons of sugar and half a teaspoon of common salt in one litre of boiled and cooled water.
texts.text: ORS—Oral Rehydration Solution— is used to treat dehydration due to diarrhoea or other illnesses. These ready-made ORS packets are available in primary health centres and also in the market. Each packet is dissolved in a litre of water before use. If these are not available, ORS can be prepared at home by mixing six teaspoons of sugar and half a teaspoon of common salt in one litre of boiled and cooled water.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Some liquids get completely mixed with water. Some do not mix with water and form a separate layer when left undisturbed for some time. Similarly, some gases are soluble in water whereas others are not. For example, oxygen gas dissolves in water. It is very important for the survival of animals and plants that live in water.
texts.text: Some liquids get completely mixed with water. Some do not mix with water and form a separate layer when left undisturbed for some time. Similarly, some gases are soluble in water whereas others are not. For example, oxygen gas dissolves in water. It is very important for the survival of animals and plants that live in water.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From Activity 6.8, we can say that any object which is heavier or lighter can be measured in terms of a property called mass.
texts.text: From Activity 6.8, we can say that any object which is heavier or lighter can be measured in terms of a property called mass.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The one which is heavier has more mass and the one which is lighter has less mass.
texts.text: The one which is heavier has more mass and the one which is lighter has less mass.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6.3.6 Space and volume
texts.text: 6.3.6 Space and volume

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Next day, Madam Vidya enters the class. All the students stand up to greet her. She reciprocates and deliberately says, "Please keep your bags on your seats and sit down." Students are not able to sit because bags are kept on their seats. Madam Vidya asks, "Why are you not sitting?" The students reply that there is no place to sit because the bags have occupied that space.
texts.text: Next day, Madam Vidya enters the class. All the students stand up to greet her. She reciprocates and deliberately says, "Please keep your bags on your seats and sit down." Students are not able to sit because bags are kept on their seats. Madam Vidya asks, "Why are you not sitting?" The students reply that there is no place to sit because the bags have occupied that space.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Continuing the conversation, she provides two identical glass tumblers to two students and encourages them to pour the remaining water from their drinking water bottles into the respective glass tumblers. On pouring water in the glass tumblers, the students observe that one glass tumbler gets halffilled with water (Fig. 6.8a) while the other is almost completely filled with water (Fig. 6.8b).
texts.text: Continuing the conversation, she provides two identical glass tumblers to two students and encourages them to pour the remaining water from their drinking water bottles into the respective glass tumblers. On pouring water in the glass tumblers, the students observe that one glass tumbler gets halffilled with water (Fig. 6.8a) while the other is almost completely filled with water (Fig. 6.8b).

texts.orig: (a) Half-filled
texts.text: (a) Half-filled

texts.label: caption

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 6.8: Glass tumblers with varying levels of water

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Why is the level of water different in the two tumblers?
texts.text: Why is the level of water different in the two tumblers?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Madam Vidya elaborates that both tumblers have the same capacity. The water levels differ in each case, which indicates that the amount of water in each tumbler is different.
texts.text: Madam Vidya elaborates that both tumblers have the same capacity. The water levels differ in each case, which indicates that the amount of water in each tumbler is different.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The water in the first tumbler occupies less space, indicating that the volume of water in this tumbler is less than the water in the other tumbler. The space occupied by water represents its volume.
texts.text: The water in the first tumbler occupies less space, indicating that the volume of water in this tumbler is less than the water in the other tumbler. The space occupied by water represents its volume.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You may have noticed drinking water bottles of different sizes being sold in the market. Have you noticed 1 L, 500 mL, 200 mL, etc., written as net quantity on the bottles? These indicate the volume of water in the bottles.
texts.text: You may have noticed drinking water bottles of different sizes being sold in the market. Have you noticed 1 L, 500 mL, 200 mL, etc., written as net quantity on the bottles? These indicate the volume of water in the bottles.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Now you have become familiar with many properties of materials. However, all materials do not possess all these properties.
texts.text: Now you have become familiar with many properties of materials. However, all materials do not possess all these properties.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6.4 What is Matter?
texts.text: 6.4 What is Matter?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mass and volume are the two properties that are possessed by all materials. Can we give a general name to anything that possesses these two properties?
texts.text: Mass and volume are the two properties that are possessed by all materials. Can we give a general name to anything that possesses these two properties?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Are there any Properties which can be shown by all materials? If yes, what are those?
texts.text: Are there any Properties which can be shown by all materials? If yes, what are those?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Anything that occupies space and has mass is called matter. The mass gives the quantity of matter, and the units to measure it are gram (g) and kilogram (kg). The space occupied by matter is its volume. The units to measure the volume are litre (L) and millilitre (mL).
texts.text: Anything that occupies space and has mass is called matter. The mass gives the quantity of matter, and the units to measure it are gram (g) and kilogram (kg). The space occupied by matter is its volume. The units to measure the volume are litre (L) and millilitre (mL).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Kilogram is the unit of mass in the International System of Units (SI). Kilogram is abbreviated in lower case as kg. There is no space between 'k' and 'g' in kg, and no full stop after the symbol, except at the end of a sentence. While writing the mass, always leave a space between the number (numerical value) and the unit. For example, if we have mass of 7 kilograms, it would be written as 7 kg and not as 7 kgs.
texts.text: Kilogram is the unit of mass in the International System of Units (SI). Kilogram is abbreviated in lower case as kg. There is no space between 'lk' and 'g' in kg, and no full stop after the symbol, except at the end of a sentence. While writing the mass, always leave a space between the number (numerical value) and the unit. For example, if we have mass of 7 kilograms, it would be written as 7 kg and not as 7 kgs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Similarly, litre is abbreviated as capital L and millilitre as mL. There is no space between 'm' and 'L' in mL. For example, if you have 500 millilitres of water, it would be written as 500 mL, m will be in lower case and L will be in upper case. The SI unit for volume is cubic metre, abbreviated as m*. The abbreviation is written with a superscript 3 to 2 denote cubic metre. For example, if you have volume of 2 cubic metres, it would be written Do you as 2 m°. Always leave a space between number know? (numerical value) and the unit. 1 m?=1000 L.
texts.text: Similarly, litre is abbreviated as capital L and millilitre as mL. There is no space between 'm' and 'L' in mL. For example, if you have 500 millilitres of water, it would be written as 500 mL, m will be in lower case and L will be in upper case. The SI unit for volume is cubic metre, abbreviated as m°. The abbreviation is written with a superscript 3 to 2 ) denote cubic metre. For example, if you have volume of 2 cubic metres, it would be written Do you as 2 m3. Always leave a space between number know? (numerical value) and the unit. 1 m?=1000 L.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Is air matter?
texts.text: Is air matter?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Can all the materials around us be considered as different examples of matter? Discuss with your friends.
texts.text: Can all the materials around us be considered as different examples of matter? Discuss with your friends.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For example, water is matter, sand and pebbles are matter and so is the cup.
texts.text: For example, water is matter, sand and pebbles are matter and so is the cup.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Materials are types of matter used in the creation or making of objects.
texts.text: Materials are types of matter used in the creation or making of objects.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We learnt that materials look different and behave differently. We grouped materials on the basis of similarities or differences in their properties.
texts.text: We learnt that materials look different and behave differently. We grouped materials on the basis of similarities or differences in their properties.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We find grouping useful as it helps us study and observe patterns in the properties of things. Humans have been classifying not only things, but also rocks, plants and animals. We have learnt about the classification of the living world in the chapter 'Diversity in the Living World'. Just like in the living world, classification of the non-living world is also done on the basis of their properties.
texts.text: We find grouping useful as it helps us study and observe patterns in the properties of things. Humans have been classifying not only things, but also rocks, plants and animals. We have learnt about the classification of the living world in the chapter 'Diversity in the Living World'. Just like in the living world, classification of the non-living world is also done on the basis of their properties.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We have explored and understood the various properties of materials.
texts.text: We have explored and understood the various properties of materials.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Yes! But most of the materials that we see today would have also existed earlier. 1am curious to know how people classified them then.
texts.text: Yes! But most of the materials that we see today would have also existed earlier. 1am curious to know how people classified them then.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A similar classification system existed in ancient India. Ayurveda, one of the Indian medical systems, too has a system of grouping things.
texts.text: A similar classification system existed in ancient India. Ayurveda, one of the Indian medical systems, too has a system of grouping things.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The shloka precisely talks about the 20 properties (guna—ten pairs of opposite properties), which are used to describe all physical matter in Ayurveda. These properties can also be used to describe all living systems (plants, animals and humans), the environment and also food.

texts.orig: Objects are made from a large variety of materials. An object can be made up of a single material or a combination of different materials.
texts.text: Objects are made from a large variety of materials. An object can be made up of a single material or a combination of different materials.

texts.text: Materials are grouped or classified based on their similarities or differences in their properties.

texts.orig: We see chairs made up of various materials, such as wood, iron, plastic, bamboo, cement and stones. Following are some desirable properties of materials which can be used to make chairs. Which materials used to make chairs fulfil these properties the most?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Gather information on different materials which can be recycled. You can take help from various sources, such as newspapers, magazines, elders in your community, and the internet.

texts.orig: Recyclers buy old objects based on properties of the materials and do not bother even if an object is broken. Conduct a survey with recyclers near you and find out what properties of materials do they check before buying objects from households. Which materials do they not buy and why?
texts.text: Recyclers buy old objects based on properties of the materials and do not bother even if an object is broken. Conduct a survey with recyclers near you and find out what properties of materials do they check before buying objects from households. Which materials do they not buy and why?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: geometric patterns, and aquatic and terrestrial animals. The clay used for making pots, dishes, bowls and other items was carefully selected and cleaned, sieved, kneaded, turned over a wheel and finally baked in kilns (baked clay is called 'terracotta'). Pots were used for various purposes, from cooking to storage of food grains, oil, ghee, and so on. Some very large storage jars and other pottery items are exhibited at the National Museum, New Delhi.
texts.text: geometric patterns, and aquatic and terrestrial animals. The clay used for making pots, dishes, bowls and other items was carefully selected and cleaned, sieved, kneaded, turned over a wheel and finally baked in kilns (baked clay is called 'terracotta'). Pots were used for various purposes, from cooking to storage of food grains, oil, ghee, and so on. Some very large storage jars and other pottery items are exhibited at the National Museum, New Delhi.
